speaker: interviewee
text: This is tape four with Kaethe Wells.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: This Sunday, that young man's name was [INAUDIBLE]. He came and the tears were rolling down his face. He said-- he didn't-- he only speak-- uh, spoke Serbian. He was a Serb. He was not a [INAUDIBLE]. And he said, they're taking the Jewish people from their homes. I've seen the so-and-so family and the so-and-so with suitcases and they're marching them to the station. And my mother said, Katy, take your father to the gypsy camp. I-- the-- there was no time to-- I said, come. We had to go through our yard, then climb a fence, go through yards, sometimes crawl on the ground, then we had to go through an open field on our belly until we saw, way back there, those colorful wagons, the gypsies. Well, one little gypsy boy saw us crawling and he must have, yeah. So we got there and I explained, we explained, and they said, we'll make a gypsy out of him. Don't worry. And I said, let us know. And my father said [INAUDIBLE]-- that was his pet name for me-- the minute it's safe, you come. I said maybe days. It was. We-- we-- we did not come for him right away. We waited several days. That was the cattle train that would-- oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I came back, I heard these loud voices. And I went in the living room, and there stood three, you know, these tall, six feet some, Gestapo-- in uniform, of course-- with my mother and my sister. And my mother looked at me and said, do you know where your father went? And I said, he took his Sunday morning walk early this morning, like he always did. Hasn't he come back yet? I'm sorry, gentlemen. But I don't know where my husband is.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I want to tell you something. If my brothers find out that the Gestapo is questioning me like I am a criminal, I don't-- they're fighting for Germany. I don't think they would like it very much, which might have helped then, but as the years went on, it didn't mean anything anymore. But it helped then. And they left and the cattle train left. And we were-- we thought we were the only ones left in Ruma, but we weren't.

speaker: interviewer
text: During that time, also the local police, the black, the Volksdeutsche police would come-- not during the day-- they came at night. At night, we got each a bucket of cold water and newspapers. You see these tanks over there? Wash them. Chicanery. One time, it was during the day, we were caught-- they came for us and took us to the church. And there was a ladder going all the way up to that little window in the-- what is it called-- church steeple. Wash it. What would have happened had you said no?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never even considered it. Never considered it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had only one thought in mind, stay alive. Lie, cheat, steal, you know, after the war-- and mother said kill-- after the war-- we did all these but killing. We said, the three of us, said to ourselves, could we have killed if we had to? We will never find out, but you know, to this day, it bothers me. Never found out. My mother said, I could have to save my children. Yes, today, I think, if it was my children, I would too. But anyway,

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do the steeple?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. No, ac-- actually, I was almost up there when I heard a commotion down below. And I wouldn't dare look down, but I heard a voice saying, Fraulein, kommen [INAUDIBLE] down. Now I backed down and there was a German lieutenant, in-- infantry lieutenant, and he said, you are to go home. We are fighting a war against the countries. We are not fighting women and children, and these people have no right to put you and-- to do this. And I said, uh, lieutenant, you are not doing us any favors because they are here to stay and you are leaving. And they're going to let us-- he said, no. I will see to it that you are not being bothered anymore. Well, famous last words. They came again at night and, uh, it went on.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long did that go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until we were then in the other-- then our father, of course, came back. And he was arrested, constantly arrested. And every time he was arrested, my mother would take the medals and the papers and go to the highest German kommandant that-- and there was a German major. And one day, mother said, never forget his name. His name is Major [? Rank ?]. He has saved my father's life again and again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: By re-- by finding out where he is and release him until the last time, when he, again, tried to have him released and he said to my mother, the Gestapo has him. They are a state within the state. The German army, the generals, they are totally helpless, can't help you. That was the last time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever give him a charge? Did they ever say why was-- they were arresting him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no, no, no, no, no, no and you never asked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they hit him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He never said that he was hit, but it was towards the end of, uh-- of '41, I believe, when a Volksdeutsche-- his name, his infamous name was Wagner, W-A-G-N-E-R. And his German was terrible. And he said to my father, you are a chemist. You make me a rich man and I'll see to it that you stay alive because you're not going to stay alive and neither is your family. You'll build me a chemical. You see, Yugoslavia, uh-- the-- the-- had no industry. It was the wine, the vineyards. And the vineyards were dying because the chemical that they needed for the grapes, there was-- there was no chemists left in Yugoslavia. Most of them were Jewish and they were long gone and my father was the only one at that time. Oh, and I forgot to tell you, he did work for the Yugoslavian ministry before the German occupation. They took him to Belgrade and brought him back again. That was a wonderful, wonderful time at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, we need [NON ENGLISH], which, translated, means copper sulfate. And you spray the grapes with. And my father said, if I say no, I'm going to die. If I say yes, I'm going to die too. The only way I'm going to do it is-- at that time, Dr. Ante Pavelic was the fuhrer of Croatia. I want a signed statement from Pavelic that neither my wife nor my children nor myself will do any more hard labor, no more arrests, leave us in peace. And it has to be signed by the highest German kommandant, which was a general, sitting in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll jump a little bit ahead. We got the paper and my father, this Wagner took him outside of Ruma, where he owned a pig farm. My father took the pig farm and built a office building and built his little lab-- well, it wasn't little. It was a lab, and hired maybe 30 people. And that's where they made the copper sulfate. And he walked there every morning. And at noon, my sister and I took turns bringing him his noon meal and walking back. That's all we did. Mother did the-- the-- the, uh, buying, the shopping for groceries. We did not leave the room we were in unless we had to. And we didn't have to, so we stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did it come that your father was later arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was still arrested. As a matter of fact, he was getting ready for work one morning, which was, like, 5:30 in summer, when-- where we lived at that time, across the street, they had brought the Gestapo headquarters from [INAUDIBLE]. That was the Gestapo head-- headquarters. To our right was the local police station and to our left was a company of German soldiers. We couldn't go to the outhouse without somebody se-- seeing us. Came this local policeman and said, doc, um, you've got to go with me. So my fa-- kissed us goodbye and my mother said, Max, you'll be home tonight. And he said, I know. I mean, it was like, goodbye. See you tomorrow. That's how my mother worked this. She immediately went to Major [? Rank ?] and he said, I'll make some phone calls, while my mother waited in his office. We weren't worried. And he said, um, none of my sources know anything about his arrest. It's not the Germans. It had to be the Croatians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the leader of the-- do you-- did you ever hear the word Ustaste? The Ustaste was for the Croatian what the Gestapo and the SS is for the Germans, only they were worse. The head of the Ustase was named Tomic, T-O-M-I-C with an accent. And he was known for his cruelty. He said Tomic is in [? Mitrovica, ?] which is 40 minutes by car, 30, 40 minutes by car.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he made more phone calls and said, yes, Tomic has him and he has been taken to [? Mitrovica. ?] So he gave my mother a chauffeur, a German soldier, in his, I guess, car, and a written permit that she was-- see, we weren't allowed, we had curfew-- that she was allowed to go to [? Mitrovica ?] and if need be, stay overnight. Now I'm going to make a long story short. She got to Mitro-- [? Mitrovica. ?] She found out that her husband, with 10 other women, Serbian women and men, were in a basement, held prisoner, and the next morning, they were to walk up that hill where they were to be shot. There were shootings going on every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness shootings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't witness any shootings. We heard the shootings, but when the Partisans killed one German-- they would-- the Germans would go from house to house and just pull them out, but they didn't shoot them. Our beautiful marketplace in the center of town, this was in the middle of summer. Mother came home one day and said, when you take your father's meal to him, go this way. Don't go this way, because one of our friends were hanging there with a dozen-- they never hung less than a dozen-- in the marketplace, in summer, in this heat. Can you imagine the sight you saw? But we did go. We had to see it for ourselves. And I see it now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Seeing such things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, nothing. Cold, cold-- just, you-- you're cold on the inside. You-- you-- you have no feelings. You have no fear. You cannot experience joy anymore. You're just cold. You're lifeless. You walk-- you-- you're like automatic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the guilt. What was the reason for the individuals who were hung? What-- of what were they guilty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Partisans, the guerrillas killed one German. They pulled him out of the house and hung them. There was-- was no explanation. One day, two German soldiers came to us. They had a room in the back of the house where they kept their library. And you said, good morning, and, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day, I heard my mother say to them, what's the matter? You look like Germany has lost the war? And he said, no, that's not it. But yesterday, he said, I don't think I'll ever sleep again. We had to go to a village-- and he mentioned the name of the village. There was not a man left in the village. They were all with the Partisans. They rounded up all the women and children, and we had to round up all the women and children, and put them in the church, barricaded the church, and then lit it on all four ends. And he said, and we had to stand there and watch it and smell it. He said, how can I go home and have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyhow, this was everyday occurrences. So my mother spent the night in [? Mitrovica ?] with the driver of Major [? Rank ?]. And the next morning-- in the meantime, she found out-- she couldn't get to him. In the meantime, she found out that they were to be shot. They were leading them up that little hill and my mother said, where is-- which one is Tomic, because there were a lot of uniforms standing there. And somebody pointed out, this is him. My mother didn't speak Serbian. And that dumb ox didn't, of course, speak any other language. She went over to him and she said, [NON ENGLISH]. And she said, you know, you do things. You don't plan to do things. There are so many things, they just come over you and you do. And she fell on her knees in front of him and grabbed his legs and said, molimo vas, molimo vas, my husband, there. And he looked down at her and helped her up and said-- she said she didn't understand a word he was saying. And one of the guys with him said, in broken German, go get your husband. He's in a good mood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she ran up the hill and said, Max, come, come. Don't ask any questions. And they ran and they ran and she shoved him in that car and said-- said to the German soldiers, take us back to Ruma. Now is something like this believable? It happened. [POINTS] It happened. When was the last time you saw your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: August 15, 1943. It was, again, a very, very hot day. And he had not left for work yet. Again, a Volksd-- a policeman came, Volksdeutsche policeman, and he seemed very uneasy. He said, doc-- pointing across the street, which means the Gestapo-- um, I'm supposed to arrest you. Why? I'm working for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You see, what happened several months before then, father came home and he seemed depressed because we-- the four of us were never depressed when we were together. We-- one tried to make the other one-- you understand what I'm trying to say? He seemed depressed and he said, I don't know what's going to happen, but they have brought in a young guy and he follows me around and he writes down everything I do. And Liesel said, oh my-- and my mother said, oh my god. When he knows how to do it, you're-- you know. And he said-- and so we said, can't you do it wrong? I mean, can't you? Children, no. And this is exactly what happened. We just didn't know when it would happen, but he finally learned how to do the copper sulfate and the Gestapo said, OK, now is the time. We have to get rid of this Jew, because from the south, the British, the White Russian Army wasn't that far away. So the policeman said-- so my father said, all right, let's go. And he said, no, doctor. Take your warmest clothes. He said, in this heat? He said, doctor-- and my mother said, oh my god. Max, take your heavy coat. He would not. So mother went and packed a suitcase and all he took was a raincoat and the suitcase. And we watched through the window. He went to the police station, not to the Gestapo, but to the police station. And my mother immediately got dressed and went to Major [? Rank ?], and that is when-- she came home about two hours later. She had to wait that long because he made all these phone calls. And that is when he said, your husband is being sent to Zagreb, and that's all we know. But I will give you a pass and railroad tickets.

speaker: interviewee
text: You get on that train today to Zagreb and you go to General-- and you know, for years and years, I've been trying to think up that General's name and I can't. He was the high commander of the German Army in Croatia. And he was in Zagreb. And he said, you go to him. You tell him that I'm sending you. You tell him the whole story. If anybody can help, it is General so-and-so. Well, we helped mother pack a little overnight bag and she went to the station. Our father was still at the pol-- at-- at the police because one of us was at the window all the time, watching to see. Finally, when noontime came, I said, he didn't eat breakfast. I'm going to fix him a sandwich and, oh my goodness, here is his shaving stuff and he hates to have a beard. I'll bring him his shaving stuff. So I fixed a basket. And when I went through the big gate in the police station, my knees started to get very rubbery. And there was a policeman standing there and I said, my father, you know, and he said.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the cell, and he was sitting on the bunk. When he saw me, he-- it was so terrible because he thought I said, mother did it again. I said, no, no. It's the Gestapo has you, and they're going to send you to Zagreb. But mother is already on a train to Zagreb to the general. I said, don't worry. It'll take a little longer this time, but-- he said, I'll be back. I'll be back. I said, eat. No, he can't eat, but he'll eat it later. I said, then shave. So in front of me, he shaved a little bit, and then we sat on the bed. And he-- he said to me at that time-- I was the last one to see him. At that time, he said to me, [CRYING] look after your mother and your sister. I said, oh no. You can do that when you come back. He said, yes, I will come back. And he said, now go. Go. He-- he couldn't-- he couldn't take it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we hugged and kissed and said, well, I'll see you soon. And he said, yes, you will see me soon. He knew he wouldn't come back. I just felt it. So when I got home, and my sister was still at the window, I said, don't leave the window. They have to take him now pretty soon. I-- I don't remember what time it was and how long we sat at the window. And all of a sudden, my father walked out of there alone with a suitcase in his hand, and he walked to the railroad station. I said, where's the Gestapo? Where's the policeman? He's walking alone. I don't understand that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We never saw him again. The next day, before mother got back, a Serbian friend came and said, what is your father doing in [INAUDIBLE]. And I said, our father was arrested by the Gestapo and he was on his way to Zagreb. I was on business in [INAUDIBLE] and all of a sudden, I couldn't believe it. There's the doctor. And I went over to him and said, [NON ENGLISH], [NON ENGLISH], what are you doing here? And my father said, I'm on my way to Zagreb. And I said, did you see any men? Nobody. They were there. They were there all the time, but that is how good they were. Even this business man said, no, he was alone. I said, no, he wasn't. They were-- the Gestapo was with him. He said, I saw him get on the train and [INAUDIBLE]. They were with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: When mother was in-- got to the general, he was in prison in Zagreb, but we never found out how long. Mother stood in front of the prison. Of course, they didn't let him in. She was with that general for hours and told him the whole life story, everything. And he closed the window and he said to her, [INAUDIBLE]. He said, I want to tell you something. I had one son and he was killed at the Battle of Stalingrad. He said, I've nothing to live for now. He said, you know Germany has lost the war. He said, we're just hanging in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, I cannot help your husband. The German Army is powerless against the Gestapo. The Gestapo is the state. They do what they want. They can have me shot tomorrow and nobody, my wife couldn't do anything about it. How old is your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in German, like I said so many times, I'm 73, but in April, I'll be 74, so I say 74. But here in America, they say you are 73. So my father, this was August. He was 59, but he would be 60 in November. So he would naturally say 60, because he's closer to 60.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so my mother said, he'll be 60 in November. And he said, he's lucky. He will never see the inside of Auschwitz. He said, I imagine this is where they're going to send him. They're waiting for a train. And he said, because when you arrive-- and he explained the whole thing to my mother-- when you arrive, your-- your age-- and-- and my mo-- and my father at that time, again, was a strong, healthy man. My mother said, they would put him to work, wouldn't they? He said, no, it goes by age. The ones 60 and over this way. They-- they never see the camp. And did they say what happened to them? He said they didn't see the camp. Did he say what would happen to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. The general said they are going to the gas chambers, straight.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they said there were gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you eventually find out what happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes and no. When mother came back, she was a shadow of herself. It was that strenuous a trip. She said, we've lost our father. He's gone. So she said, now I have another job to do, to keep you girls alive. That December, an old Serbian woman came to the door and she spoke a Serbian dialect that I couldn't understand, but my sister had a-- my sister was fantastic when it came to languages. But she had trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she had a postcard in her hand and said to my sister, read it. And on this pos-- postcard, translated, it says, dear mother-- the postcard came from a prison in [? Tyrolea, ?] Tyrol. And it says, dear mother, you know I've been taken to work in Germany. Do you remember the workforce that the Germans all from the occupied countries, they took the young people into Ger-- to work? Well, this young guy from Ruma was taken because he was young, and he was working on a farm in wherever it was. He said, you know, I've been working on a farm, but I've been a bad boy. And she then told us what happened to her son.
